import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.axes import Axes

# Set random seed for reproducibility
np.random.seed(19680801)

# Generate data for Gaussian colored noise
# Using an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process to simulate colored noise
n_points = 1000
t = np.linspace(0, 1, n_points)
dt = t[1] - t[0]
theta = 5.0  # correlation time inverse
mu = 0.0
sigma = 1.0

# Generate white noise
white_noise = np.random.normal(mu, sigma, n_points)

# Generate colored noise (Ornstein-Uhlenbeck)
colored_noise = np.zeros(n_points)
for i in range(1, n_points):
    colored_noise[i] = colored_noise[i-1] + (-theta * colored_noise[i-1] * dt) + (sigma * np.sqrt(dt) * white_noise[i])

# Scale to reasonable current values (nA)
current_data = colored_noise * 0.5

# Generate data for Impulse response (simple exponential decay)
t_impulse = np.linspace(0, 0.2, 200)
impulse_response = np.exp(-10 * t_impulse) * np.sin(20 * t_impulse)

# Create the main figure and axis
fig, ax_main = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 6))

# Plot main data
ax_main.plot(t, current_data, color='blue', linewidth=1)
ax_main.set_xlabel('Time (s)')
ax_main.set_ylabel('Current (nA)')
ax_main.set_title('Gaussian colored noise')

# Set y-limits with padding
y_min, y_max = np.min(current_data), np.max(current_data)
y_padding = (y_max - y_min) * 0.05
ax_main.set_ylim(y_min - y_padding, y_max + y_padding)

# Create first inset (Right) - Histogram
# Position: [left, bottom, width, height] in figure coordinates
# Placing on the right side inside the main plot area
ax_inset_right = fig.add_axes([0.65, 0.55, 0.25, 0.25])
ax_inset_right.hist(current_data, bins=30, density=True, color='white', edgecolor='white', alpha=0.7)
ax_inset_right.set_facecolor('black')
ax_inset_right.tick_params(axis='both', which='both', length=0, labelsize=0)
ax_inset_right.spines['top'].set_visible(False)
ax_inset_right.spines['right'].set_visible(False)
ax_inset_right.spines['bottom'].set_visible(False)
ax_inset_right.spines['left'].set_visible(False)

# Create second inset (Left) - Impulse response
# Placing on the left side inside the main plot area
ax_inset_left = fig.add_axes([0.15, 0.55, 0.25, 0.25])
ax_inset_left.plot(t_impulse, impulse_response, color='white', linewidth=1)
ax_inset_left.set_facecolor('black')
ax_inset_left.set_xlim(0, 0.2)
ax_inset_left.tick_params(axis='both', which='both', length=0, labelsize=0)
ax_inset_left.spines['top'].set_visible(False)
ax_inset_right.spines['right'].set_visible(False)
ax_inset_left.spines['bottom'].set_visible(False)
ax_inset_left.spines['left'].set_visible(False)

# Save the figure
plt.savefig("/tmp/docviz_b1_renders/plot2code_00_plot2code.png", dpi=120, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.close('all')

import os
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches

def create_treemap_data():
    """
    Generates hard-coded hierarchical data mimicking the structure described.
    Returns two datasets: one full hierarchy, one for maxdepth=1 simulation.
    """
    # Dataset 1: Full Hierarchy (ids, labels, parents)
    # Structure: Root -> Category -> Subcategory -> Item
    data_full = [
        {"id": "root", "label": "Total", "parent": "", "value": 100},
        {"id": "cat_a", "label": "Category A", "parent": "root", "value": 60},
        {"id": "cat_b", "label": "Category B", "parent": "root", "value": 40},
        {"id": "sub_a1", "label": "Sub A1", "parent": "cat_a", "value": 35},
        {"id": "sub_a2", "label": "Sub A2", "parent": "cat_a", "value": 25},
        {"id": "sub_b1", "label": "Sub B1", "parent": "cat_b", "value": 20},
        {"id": "sub_b2", "label": "Sub B2", "parent": "cat_b", "value": 20},
        {"id": "item_a1a", "label": "Item A1a", "parent": "sub_a1", "value": 20},
        {"id": "item_a1b", "label": "Item A1b", "parent": "sub_a1", "value": 15},
        {"id": "item_a2a", "label": "Item A2a", "parent": "sub_a2", "value": 15},
        {"id": "item_a2b", "label": "Item A2b", "parent": "sub_a2", "value": 10},
        {"id": "item_b1a", "label": "Item B1a", "parent": "sub_b1", "value": 12},
        {"id": "item_b1b", "label": "Item B1b", "parent": "sub_b1", "value": 8},
        {"id": "item_b2a", "label": "Item B2a", "parent": "sub_b2", "value": 10},
        {"id": "item_b2b", "label": "Item B2b", "parent": "sub_b2", "value": 10},
    ]

    # Dataset 2: Flattened for Max Depth 1
    # In a real scenario, this would be filtered. Here we simulate the result
    # where only the immediate children of the root are visible as top-level blocks.
    data_flat = [
        {"id": "root", "label": "Total", "parent": "", "value": 100},
        {"id": "cat_a", "label": "Category A", "parent": "root", "value": 60},
        {"id": "cat_b", "label": "Category B", "parent": "root", "value": 40},
    ]
    
    return data_full, data_flat

def build_hierarchy_map(data):
    """Converts list of dicts into a tree structure and calculates relative areas."""
    lookup = {item["id"]: item for item in data}
    children = {item["id"]: [] for item in data}
    
    for item in data:
        pid = item["parent"]
        if pid in children:
            children[pid].append(item["id"])
            
    return lookup, children

def calculate_rects(node_id, lookup, children, x0, y0, width, height):
    """
    Recursive Squarified-like (simplified slice-and-dice for stability without numpy) 
    or simple vertical/horizontal split to generate rectangles.
    Using a simple alternating split strategy for pure python implementation.
    """
    item = lookup[node_id]
    label = item["label"]
    
    # Get children
    child_ids = children.get(node_id, [])
    if not child_ids:
        return [(x0, y0, width, height, label)]
    
    # Calculate total value of children to normalize
    total_val = sum(lookup[cid]["value"] for cid in child_ids)
    if total_val == 0:
        return [(x0, y0, width, height, label)]
    
    rects = []
    current_x = x0
    current_y = y0
    
    # Simple horizontal stacking for demonstration of hierarchy
    # Sort children by value descending for better visualization
    child_ids_sorted = sorted(child_ids, key=lambda k: lookup[k]["value"], reverse=True)
    
    # We will split the current box based on the proportion of values
    # Using a simple row-based approach for clarity in pure python
    
    # Determine orientation based on depth (even=horizontal, odd=vertical)
    # Depth calculation
    depth = 0
    curr = node_id
    while lookup[curr]["parent"] != "":
        depth += 1
        curr = lookup[curr]["parent"]
    
    horizontal = (depth % 2 == 0)
    
    remaining_w = width
    remaining_h = height
    
    for i, cid in enumerate(child_ids_sorted):
        val = lookup[cid]["value"]
        ratio = val / total_val
        
        if horizontal:
            w = width * ratio
            h = height
            # Recurse
            rects.extend(calculate_rects(cid, lookup, children, x0 + (width - remaining_w), y0, w, h))
            remaining_w -= w
        else:
            w = width
            h = height * ratio
            # Recurse
            rects.extend(calculate_rects(cid, lookup, children, x0, y0 + (height - remaining_h), w, h))
            remaining_h -= h
            
    return rects

def draw_treemap(ax, data, title):
    lookup, children = build_hierarchy_map(data)
    root_id = "root"
    
    # Generate rectangles
    # Start with full unit square
    rects = calculate_rects(root_id, lookup, children, 0, 0, 1, 1)
    
    # Filter out the root label if it covers everything, or style it differently
    # In treemaps, usually the root is the container, we plot children.
    # Our recursive function returns leaf containers or intermediate if no children.
    # Let's refine: The recursive function above returns the bounding boxes of the lowest level visited?
    # No, it returns the boxes for the children. Wait, the logic above recurses down.
    # It returns a list of (x, y, w, h, label) for the *leaf* nodes of the visualization.
    # But we want to show all levels.
    
    # Let's rewrite the drawing logic to collect ALL visible nodes, not just leaves.
    # We need a traversal that yields (x, y, w, h, label) for every node we want to draw.
    
    ax.clear()
    ax.set_xlim(0, 1)
    ax.set_ylim(0, 1)
    ax.set_aspect('equal')
    ax.axis('off')
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=12, pad=10)
    
    def traverse_and_draw(nid, x, y, w, h, depth):
        # Draw current node background
        # Skip drawing the absolute root if it covers the whole plot and has children, 
        # or draw it as a border. Let's draw all except the virtual root if it's just a container.
        # However, the prompt says "Full Hierarchy". Usually root is the frame.
        
        item = lookup[nid]
        label = item["label"]
        
        # Only draw if it's not the invisible root or if we want to show root label
        if nid != "root":
            rect = mpatches.Rectangle((x, y), w, h, linewidth=1, edgecolor='white', facecolor=plt.cm.Blues((depth % 3 + 1) / 4.0))
            ax.add_patch(rect)
            # Add text
            if w > 0.05 and h > 0.05: # Only text if big enough
                ax.text(x + w/2, y + h/2, label, ha='center', va='center', fontsize=8, color='black')
        
        child_ids = children.get(nid, [])
        if not child_ids:
            return

        total_val = sum(lookup[cid]["value"] for cid in child_ids)
        if total_val == 0: return
        
        child_ids_sorted = sorted(child_ids, key=lambda k: lookup[k]["value"], reverse=True)
        
        horizontal = (depth % 2 == 0)
        
        curr_offset = 0
        
        for cid in child_ids_sorted:
            val = lookup[cid]["value"]
            ratio = val / total_val
            
            if horizontal:
                cw = w * ratio
                ch = h
                traverse_and_draw(cid, x + curr_offset, y, cw, ch, depth + 1)
                curr_offset += cw
            else:
                cw = w
                ch = h * ratio
                traverse_and_draw(cid, x, y + curr_offset, cw, ch, depth + 1)
                curr_offset += ch

    traverse_and_draw(root_id, 0, 0, 1, 1, 0)

def main():
    # Ensure output directory exists
    os.makedirs("/tmp/docviz_b1_renders", exist_ok=True)
    
    data_full, data_flat = create_treemap_data()
    
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 6))
    fig.suptitle("Side-by-Side Treemap Comparison", fontsize=16, fontweight='bold')
    
    # Left Plot: Full Hierarchy
    draw_treemap(axes[0], data_full, "Treemap 1: Full Hierarchy")
    
    # Right Plot: Max Depth 1 (Simulated by passing flattened data)
    draw_treemap(axes[1], data_flat, "Treemap 2: Max Depth 1")
    
    plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0.03, 1, 0.95])
    
    save_path = "/tmp/docviz_b1_renders/plot2code_02_plot2code.png"
    plt.savefig(save_path, dpi=120, bbox_inches="tight")
    plt.close('all')

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

# Data from United Nations World Population Prospects (Revision 2019)

years = [1950, 1960, 1970, 1980, 1990, 2000, 2010, 2018]

# Population in millions for each continent
# Order: Africa, Americas, Asia, Europe, Oceania
africa = [221, 285, 361, 478, 631, 814, 1044, 1276]
americas = [339, 421, 514, 616, 720, 834, 942, 1002]
asia = [1402, 1706, 2143, 2632, 3100, 3721, 4167, 4561]
europe = [547, 605, 656, 692, 721, 727, 738, 747]
oceania = [13, 16, 19, 22, 27, 31, 37, 41]

# Stack order: Bottom to Top -> Africa, Americas, Asia, Europe, Oceania
# To reverse the legend relative to stacking, we list them in reverse order in the legend call
# or handle the labels accordingly. The prompt says "legend order is reversed relative to the stacking order".
# Stacking order (bottom-up): Africa, Americas, Asia, Europe, Oceania.
# Legend order should be: Oceania, Europe, Asia, Americas, Africa.

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))

# Plot stacked areas

africa = np.array(africa)
americas = np.array(americas)
asia = np.array(asia)
europe = np.array(europe)
oceania = np.array(oceania)

# Cumulative bases
base_africa = np.zeros(len(years))
base_americas = africa
base_asia = africa + americas
base_europe = africa + americas + asia
base_oceania = africa + americas + asia + europe

# Plotting with default color cycle and alpha=0.8
plt.fill_between(years, base_africa, base_africa + africa, label='Africa', alpha=0.8)
plt.fill_between(years, base_americas, base_americas + americas, label='Americas', alpha=0.8)
plt.fill_between(years, base_asia, base_asia + asia, label='Asia', alpha=0.8)
plt.fill_between(years, base_europe, base_europe + europe, label='Europe', alpha=0.8)
plt.fill_between(years, base_oceania, base_oceania + oceania, label='Oceania', alpha=0.8)

# Labels and Title
plt.xlabel("Year")
plt.ylabel("Number of people (millions)")
plt.title("World population")

# X-axis ticks
plt.xticks(years)

# Legend: Reverse order relative to stacking (Stack: Africa->Oceania, Legend: Oceania->Africa)
